chunks.title: Catalog Description
chunks.content:
Distributed Systems. (3-0). Credit 3. Principles and techniques for engineering
distributed systems with topics including communication, concurrency,
programming paradigms, naming, managing shared state, caching,
synchronization, reaching agreement, fault tolerance, security, middleware and
distributed applications; design, implement and debug large software systems.
Prerequisite: CSCE 313; junior or senior classification, or approval of instructor.

chunks.title: Additional Course Details
chunks.content:
The objectives for this course are for students to gain an understanding of the principles and practices
(e.g., locking, concurrency, scheduling, communication across networks, fault tolerance, protocols,
remote procedure calls, file sharing, reliable system design, load balancing, protection and security)
behind the design of distributed processing systems, such as Google, Facebook, Amazon, Yahoo and
Microsoft, and to gain practical experience designing, implementing, and debugging distributed
systems.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content:
Prerequisite/Corequisite(s): CSCE 313; junior or senior classification, or approval of
instructor.
Restrictions: None

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:
• Implement and structure distributed system programs that operate using well-
defined protocols
• Write computer programs that interoperate using well-defined protocols
• Employ and create common paradigms (remote procedure calls and message
oriented middleware) for easing the task of distributed systems programming
• Debug highly concurrent code that spans multiple programs running on multiple
machines
• Reason about distributed algorithms for locking, synchronization and concurrency,
scheduling, and replication

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
This material Is: Required
Distributed Systems
Authors: Maarten van Steen, Andrew S. Tanenbaum
Publication Date: January 2025
Edition: 4.0.3
URL for Resource: https://www.distributed-systems.net/index.php/books/ds4/

This material Is: Required
Research papers distributed in class

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
Homework: 10%
Machine Problems (MPs): 40%
Midterm Exam 1: 15%
Midterm Exam 2: 15%
Final Exam: 20%

The grading scale will be: A ≥ 90% > B ≥ 80% > C ≥ 70% > D ≥ 60% > F.

Assignments: there will be 5 Machine Problems (MPs) involving C/C++ and Java programming. There
will be 6 homework assignments. The exams will be in class, closed book, closed notes, pencil-paper
based, with a "cheat-sheet" provided by the instructor. The Final Exam is cumulative, with 80% of the
questions covering material from the Midterm 2.

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not
considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Course Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
One Machine Problem (MP) submission will be accepted with no penalty for up to 4 days late. The
allowable 4 late days cannot be split among multiple Machine Problems. Allowable late days are for
emergencies. All other late Machine Problems will be accepted with a penalty of 20% per late day.
No late submissions will be accepted for homework.

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content:
According to the Texas A&M University Definitions of Academic Misconduct, plagiarism is the
appropriation of another person's ideas, processes, results or words without giving appropriate
credit (aggiehonor.tamu.edu). You should credit your use of anyone else's words, graphic images,
or ideas using standard citation styles. Artificial Intelligence (AI) text generators and natural
language processing tools (colloquially, chatbots - such as ChatGPT), audio, computer code,
video, and image generators should not be used for any work for this class without explicit
permission of the instructor and appropriate attribution. This includes, but is not limited to, i)
Creating or revising drafts; ii) Editing your work; iii) Reviewing a peer's work. This excludes
pre-existing software additions such as spelling and grammar checkers, which are acceptable.
